I was driving along Circum this morning when the stoplight turned red. A garbage truck was right in front of us. 

Two men were standing on the open bed, shifting big trash bags with their hands. No gloves. No masks. No boots. Just ordinary green long sleeve shirt (I'm assuming it's their uniform) and pants (one was wearing what looks like a pajama) and worn-out shoes while handling garbage.

My wife and I started talking about them right there at the light. Why are they working with zero protection? Is it just the heat? Walang supply? Or is there something much worse behind that one snapshot? 

I went home wanting to answer that simple question. After a few hours of reading, the question changed. It was no longer "Why don't they have PPE?" It became: "Why are they treated as if they barely exist in the system at all?"

I also have to admit I'm part of this story. My street, my neighbor, and the way people like us treat garbage collectors all shape the world these workers move in every day.

 MY STREET, MY NEIGHBOR, AND THE "BASURERO" THEY AVOIDED

For years, garbage collection on our street was a mess—sometimes literally. One big reason: one of my neighbors.

She hates these guys with a passion. Every time the truck passed, you could see it in her face: disgust, like the collectors were the scum of the earth. Parang sila ang dumi, hindi 'yung basura. She complained when they were noisy, complained when they came late, and complained when they came at all.

Over time, that kind of attitude sends a signal. You are not welcome here. You're just tolerated at best.

Last year, I decided I didn't want to live on that kind of street.

I started small:

- I gave tips to the garbage collectors when they came around.
- I greeted them "Good morning" instead of pretending they were invisible.
- I handed them bread or snacks whenever I could.

My goal was simple: to show them that at least one house on this street actually wants them there, that they are not a nuisance, that they are doing a job we depend on. Every December, we prepared gifts and a little bonus for them.

And I'll be honest again: back then, I was doing it more out of basic kindness and Christmas spirit than any deep understanding. 

I still assumed, in the back of my mind, that they were regular city employees. 

Akala ko, may benefits sila. Akala ko, may hazard pay at GSIS at kung anu-ano pa. I thought I was just being nice on top of what the system was already giving them.

I was wrong.

This research showed me that for many garbage collectors, those December gifts and small tips are not "extra." They are sometimes the closest thing they get to a bonus, to being treated as human beings who matter.

 THE JOB: HARD, DIRTY, AND DANGEROUS — WITH THE DATA TO PROVE IT

When you stop romanticizing "mahirap ang trabaho nila" and actually read the numbers, it gets uncomfortable fast.

Studies on Filipino garbage collectors show:

- 39.55% suffer from musculoskeletal problems—chronic pain in the back, shoulders, knees, and neck from constant lifting and jumping on and off trucks.
- 21.54% have gastrointestinal problems like ulcers, diarrhea, and stomach pain.
- 12.86% have skin diseases such as rashes and infections.
- Another study found that around 19% have persistent respiratory symptoms like chronic cough and shortness of breath.

In short: almost 4 out of 10 collectors are living with serious body pain, 2 out of 10 with stomach issues, 1 out of 10 with skin problems, and 2 with breathing trouble. That's the baseline, not the worst case.

And they are dealing with:

- Hidden sharps (broken glass, needles, rusty metal) inside sealed bags.
- Medical and sanitary waste mixed into ordinary trash.
- Rotting organic waste full of bacteria, maggots, insects, and vermin.

Now imagine doing that in slippers or thin shoes, bare-handed, on the back of a moving truck.

 THE PAY: ESSENTIAL WORK, NEAR-MINIMUM WAGES

Then you check what they earn.

Based on available salary data:

- An entry-level garbage collector (1–3 years) earns about ₱131,256 a year, or roughly ₱10,900 a month.
- A senior garbage collector (8+ years) earns about ₱198,811 a year, or roughly ₱16,500 a month.

This is for full-time work that damages their bodies and exposes them to disease. It's not far from minimum wage, and it doesn't automatically come with full benefits.

For informal waste pickers—those outside the formal system—things are worse. 

A 67-year-old waste picker featured in one story earns about ₱300 a day on good days. If he works 20 days in a month, that's ₱6,000—with no health insurance, no GSIS, no retirement, no hazard pay, nothing to catch him if he gets sick and can't work.

I used to think the people on the truck outside my house had "regular" government compensation behind them. Now I know that many of them do not.

 THE STATUS: THE CONTRACT THAT KEEPS THEM DISPOSABLE

Here's the part I really didn't understand before: legally, a lot of these workers are almost invisible.

In many LGUs, garbage collectors are hired as Job Order (JO) or Contract of Service (COS) workers. That classification sounds like paperwork. It's actually a wall.

As JO/COS, they:

- Do not have a civil service appointment.
- Are explicitly labeled "not government employees."
- Are not covered by Civil Service Law protections.
- Work under short contracts that can simply not be renewed.

So while we see them riding LGU trucks and assume "empleyado 'yan ng city," the law often treats them more like temporary service providers, even if they have been doing the same job for 10 or 15 years.

Regular government employees, on the other hand:

- Have appointments approved by the Civil Service Commission.
- Are compulsorily covered by GSIS (life insurance, disability, retirement).
- Get paid leave, 13th-month pay, performance bonuses, and clearer grievance mechanisms.

The men on the truck in front of my car? Many like them are stuck in that JO/COS limbo.

 PROTECTION ON PAPER, EXPOSURE ON THE GROUND

Our laws actually sound protective.

- The Ecological Solid Waste Management Act says sanitation workers should be given gloves, masks, and safety boots.
- Occupational safety laws say employers must provide PPE for free and train workers on how to handle risks.

But audits of real cities show:

- Hundreds of garbage collectors doing their work with inconsistent or no PPE.
- Some barangay-level collectors with zero protective equipment—no gloves, no masks, no boots.
- City and private hauler workers who have never received formal training on safe garbage collection, despite requirements.

So yes, the law talks about safety. But JO status plus weak enforcement means the people on the ground often see none of it.

 MENTAL HEALTH AND STIGMA: THE WEIGHT WE DON'T SEE

There's also the mental side of this work.

One study found that about 27.4% of informal waste workers show signs of depression. The risk is higher for older workers, those who are already sick, those with unstable income, and those with no savings.

On top of that, they face daily disrespect. People wrinkle their noses. Some neighbors talk about them like they're the problem. Kids learn this attitude from adults. It becomes normal to treat the people who handle our trash as if they are the trash.

When I think of my neighbor—how she looks at garbage collectors like they're not even worth basic courtesy—I see that moment in a very different light now. I used to just be annoyed at her. Now I see how that contempt is part of the environment these workers move through every day.

 THERE ARE BETTER MODELS: PASIG SHOWS IT'S A CHOICE

This is where Pasig City changes the conversation.

Since 2019, Pasig has made a deliberate effort to regularize long-time contractual workers:

- By 2022, they had regularized 2,397 employees, many of whom had been casuals for 10–20 years.
- By late 2025, the city reported over 5,000 employees regularized since 2019. Permanent employees now outnumber casuals and JO workers.
- Street sweepers and other low-paid frontline staff are among those moved into permanent positions, with full benefits and security of tenure.

Pasig still outsources some garbage collection, so it's not a perfect story. But it proves this: the current system is not inevitable. An LGU that wants to can:

- Create new permanent positions.
- Prioritize long-serving JO workers for those slots.
- Give them real appointments and full protection.

That's a policy choice. Which means not doing it is also a choice.

 HOW THIS CHANGED MY PERSPECTIVE

Before this morning, I thought I was already "doing my part" in a small way—greeting the collectors, giving tips, handing out bread, preparing a Christmas envelope every December. I thought I was just adding kindness on top of a stable foundation.

Now I see it differently.

- I can no longer assume they're covered just because they wear a green or orange LGU shirt.
- I understand that many of them are one bad injury away from losing both their job and their only income.
- I see that my street, my neighbor's looks, and my own silence are all part of the environment they walk into every time the truck turns into our road.

This research didn't just give me numbers; it gave me a clearer picture of how little protection stands between these workers and disaster. And it forced me to admit that small acts of kindness are good, but they're not enough.

 A PRAYER AND A PLEA: FOR LAWMAKERS AND FOR US

So here's where I am now.

I'm praying—literally—that our lawmakers will prioritize the Magna Carta or any serious law that protects waste workers:

- That Congress will stop allowing essential workers to hide under JO/COS labels.
- That the Senate will push through legislation that guarantees decent pay, hazard allowances, PPE, medical checkups, and real retirement for people who have spent their lives handling our waste.
- That LGUs will be pushed, or required, to follow models like Pasig's, not just when it's politically convenient but as a matter of justice.

And I'm also praying that more ordinary people will help our waste handlers in ways both small and systemic:

- Greet them. Look them in the eye. Let them know they are welcome on your street, not a nuisance.
- If you can, give water, bread, or a small tip. It doesn't fix the system, but it tells them someone sees them.
- Use your voice with your barangay, city hall, and representatives. Bring up waste workers when people talk about "frontliners" and "essential services."

Because they need our help too—not just with a Christmas envelope or a random loaf of bread, but with a louder demand that the laws and budgets finally match the importance of the work they do.

This all started with a red light, a garbage truck, and two men in front of my car. I can't change what they're paid or how they're classified with a snap of my fingers. But I can refuse to look away. I can write about them. I can keep saying to anyone in power who will listen:

Look at them. They're holding up a part of this country that none of us want to touch. The least we can do is hold up our end.

 Nota Bene: This piece was supposed to be written in Tagalog, as my wife suggested, but when I read through what ended up on the page, I realized I’m not really talking to the garbage collectors themselves. I’m talking to you, to us, and to the lawmakers who decide how their lives will go from here. 

My hope is that this doesn’t just become another story that people nod along to and forget, but a kind of written prayer that does not fall on deaf ears—that something moves in Congress and in city halls, that this new-found advocacy turns into concrete protection for our waste handlers, and that more and more of us choose to stand with them instead of looking away.

To: MCT

From: VY

Subject: Help us understand what's going on at DFA, Customs, and the Palace

Hello MCT,

Thank you for all that you do for our country.

I humbly request that you help uncover what's going on in DFA, Customs. 

Love to know your perspective on its department secretary and the radio silence amidst all the PH-PRC discourse and PRC disrespect of our officials. 

To add insult to injury, they grant 14-day visa free stay here in our country. As a Filipino, I feel the injustice everyday and no firm and clear denouncement from DFA and the palace. 

Please shine light on this and help our fellowmen understand these issues to better hold our government officials more accountable. Maraming salamat po!

God bless you and your family.

Sincerely,

VY

 From MCT: 

Maraming salamat, VY.

Your email cuts straight to the heart of what so many Filipinos are feeling right now. The frustration. The anger. The sense that something is deeply wrong with how our government is handling China.

You asked me to help shine light on what's happening at the DFA, the radio silence from the Palace, and that 14-day visa-free policy that feels like a slap in the face while Chinese vessels are harassing our fishermen and their embassy is attacking our officials by name.

Let me try to make sense of this mess. 

Please note - I am not an expert. This is just me thinking out loud based on facts I see, so please take this response with a grain of salt and do additional research in case anything is amiss.

 WHAT'S ACTUALLY HAPPENING

Here's what we know.

From early January until now, the Chinese Embassy in Manila has launched at least 15 Facebook posts targeting Philippine officials by name. 

Not diplomatic notes passed quietly through proper channels. 

Public attacks. On social media. 

Calling out Commodore Jay Tarriela of the Philippine Coast Guard, Senator Kiko Pangilinan, Representative Leila de Lima, Representative Chel Diokno, and the National Maritime Council.

They accused Tarriela of "maliciously spreading disinformation" and demanded the government hold him accountable.

When he used AI-generated caricatures in a presentation to students, the embassy filed "solemn representations" with Malacañang, the DFA, and the PCG, calling it a "serious violation of China's political dignity."

They told Pangilinan to "comprehend the history, facts, and legal context" before speaking about China's military drills near Taiwan.

They dismissed De Lima's statements as "wrong and groundless."

They told Diokno his "logic is fundamentally erroneous" after he invoked the Vienna Convention on Diplomatic Relations, the international law that says foreign diplomats can't interfere in a host country's internal affairs.

On January 20, Chinese Foreign Ministry spokesperson Guo Jiakun warned that Philippine officials who continue their "provocations" would "pay the price."

On January 22, China summoned our ambassador in Beijing and demanded that Manila "undo the negative impact" caused by Tarriela's statements.

A foreign embassy. Attacking Filipino government officials. By name. On Facebook. And warning them they'll "pay the price."

Bastos. Walang respeto. Disrespectful doesn't even begin to cover it.

 THE DFA'S RESPONSE: TOO LITTLE, TOO LATE

So what did the Department of Foreign Affairs do?

Nothing. 

It wasn't until January 21, after Senator Risa Hontiveros sent a letter to DFA Secretary Ma. Theresa Lazaro demanding action, that the department finally issued a statement.

And even then, ang hina. 

The statement was vague, lukewarm, and avoided naming the Chinese Embassy directly. 

The DFA said it "emphasizes its support for elected officials and government entities fulfilling their legal responsibilities in safeguarding Philippine sovereignty" and stressed that "differences between states are best addressed through established diplomatic channels rather than public exchanges."

That's it. That's the whole response.

No clear rebuke of China's interference. No defense of Tarriela or the other officials under attack. No consequences. 

Hontiveros was right to call this out. 

"Binabastos na nga ng Tsina ang ating karagatan, binabastos pa ang mga opisyal natin," she said. China is already disrespecting our waters, and now they're disrespecting our officials too. She warned that the DFA's silence "risks normalizing foreign interference and undermining the dignity of Philippine institutions."

Political analyst Renato De Castro put it even more bluntly: the DFA is "trained for state-to-state engagement and not for confronting public-facing attacks." 

In other words, when China takes the gloves off and goes public with its attacks, the DFA doesn't know how to fight back.

 THE PALACE: RADIO SILENCE

And the Palace? Malacañang?

Nothing. Zero. Zilch. Nil. Wala. Itlog. Bunay. Sugok. Salag. 

Walang statement.

Claire Castro, the Palace press officer, held a briefing on January 21. She talked about President Marcos' health, the peso exchange rate, and impeachment complaints. 

Not a single word about the Chinese Embassy's attacks on Filipino officials.

This is the same president who, in March 2024, issued a strong statement after a water cannon attack on our Coast Guard, saying "Filipinos do not yield."

Now? Silence.

That silence is deafening. And it sends a message: that we're willing to tolerate public disrespect from a foreign power. That our officials are on their own when China comes after them. That sovereignty is negotiable.

 THE 14-DAY VISA-FREE POLICY: BAD TIMING OR SURRENDER?

Now here's where it gets even more absurd.

On January 15, the DFA announced that starting January 16, Chinese nationals would be allowed to enter the Philippines without a visa for up to 14 days. 

The policy is non-extendible, non-convertible, and only applies at NAIA Manila and Mactan-Cebu airports.

The rationale? To boost tourism, trade, and investment. To strengthen "people-to-people ties" between the Philippines and China.

Let's talk about timing.

This announcement came in the middle of the Chinese Embassy's three-week assault on Philippine officials. 

It came days after the first harassment incident of 2026, when Chinese Coast Guard and PLA Navy vessels blocked and harassed a Filipino fishing boat near Scarborough Shoal on January 13. 

It came while China was tightening its grip on the West Philippine Sea, increasing patrols, and warning Filipino fishermen to stay away.

And, and this is important, it came with no reciprocity. China has not offered similar visa-free access to Filipinos.

The government says this is "purely economic." 

But as VERA Files pointed out, "a visa policy does not exist in a vacuum; it communicates intentions as much as it grants privileges." 

When Chinese vessels are firing water cannons at our Coast Guard, blocking our fishermen, and summoning our ambassador, offering visa-free entry "without parallel de-escalation or confidence-building from Beijing raises questions about leverage used too lightly."

On Reddit, Filipinos were furious. One post summed it up: "China fires water cannons onto PH coast guard. PH government response: Gives Chinese visa free status. Peak stupid."

Tama sila. It’s fucking stupid.

 WHAT'S HAPPENING IN THE WEST PHILIPPINE SEA

Because this isn't just about diplomatic rhetoric. There are real consequences. Real people being hurt.

On January 13, the Philippine Coast Guard logged the first harassment incident of 2026. 

A Chinese Coast Guard vessel and a PLA Navy warship approached within 30 meters of the Filipino fishing boat Prince LJ near Scarborough Shoal, blocked its path, and blared sirens. 

The incident lasted from noon until 3 p.m. The PCG had to respond and provide 10 containers of diesel so the fishermen could continue.

Rear Admiral Roy Vincent Trinidad called it what it is: "coercive and aggressive actions designed to destroy the will to fish of our fishermen."

This has been happening for years.

Since 2012, when China effectively seized Scarborough Shoal, Filipino fishermen have been harassed, intimidated, and displaced. 

Their catch has dropped by half. Some have developed psychological trauma and refuse to go back to sea.

Former Zambales fisherfolk leader Elsa Novo said it plainly: "They did not overturn just one pot, but many pots. So many families will go hungry because of what they are doing."

These are Filipino families. Filipino livelihoods. Being destroyed. While our government stays silent and rolls out visa-free policies for Chinese nationals.

 THE PATTERN: CONTRADICTIONS ALL THE WAY DOWN

Here's what doesn't make sense.

President Marcos came into office in 2022 pledging to uphold the 2016 arbitral ruling that declared China's nine-dash line invalid and affirmed Philippine sovereignty. 

He strengthened ties with the United States, Japan, and Australia. He embedded media in Coast Guard missions so the world could see China's aggression. He filed 247 diplomatic protests since July 2022.

But he also tried to "compartmentalize" the disputes with China, seeking economic cooperation while managing maritime tensions. He kept the door open for negotiation.

And now, in January 2026, with tensions at a boiling point, the administration grants visa-free entry to Chinese nationals. 

While the Chinese Embassy is publicly attacking Filipino officials. 

While Chinese vessels are harassing our fishermen. 

While our ambassador is being summoned in Beijing and told to "rein in" our own Coast Guard spokesperson.

Some say he is balancing, but what he is doing is sending mixed signals so confusing that no one, not Beijing, not Washington, not even Filipinos at home, knows what the policy actually is.

As one analyst put it, "Diplomacy without deterrence is weakness."

 WHAT THIS ALL MEANS

So what's going on at the DFA? At Customs? At the Palace?

Here's my take.

The DFA is outmatched. 

Secretary Ma. Theresa Lazaro took over from Enrique Manalo in July 2025. 

She's a career diplomat who led the Bilateral Consultation Mechanism negotiations with China. 

But the DFA is built for traditional state-to-state diplomacy, not for public confrontations on social media. 

When China goes on the offensive with wolf warrior tactics, the DFA doesn't have the tools or the backbone to fight back.

The Palace is prioritizing economic stability over sovereignty. 

The peso is sliding toward P60:$1. 
Tourism revenue is down. 

The administration wants Chinese tourists and investment. 

So they're willing to tolerate disrespect, harassment, and threats if it means keeping the economic relationship alive.

Customs (BOC) isn't the main issue here. BOC collected P934.4 billion in 2025, missing its target. 

There are ongoing issues with smuggling and misdeclared goods, but the bigger story is the contradiction in policy: 

- we banned POGOs in 2024 because of Chinese criminal syndicates, 

- deported hundreds of Chinese nationals linked to scams and trafficking, 

..and then turned around and gave visa-free entry to Chinese tourists.

WTF, right? 🤷🏽‍♂️

The administration doesn't know how to respond to public disrespect. 

When China attacks our officials on Facebook, the Palace stays silent. 

When China summons our ambassador, we don't summon theirs. 

When China demands we "rein in" Tarriela, the DFA issues a vague statement that doesn't even name China.

This is what appeasement looks like. And it's happening in real time.

 WHAT SHOULD HAPPEN

So what should the government do?

The DFA should have responded immediately when the Chinese Embassy started attacking Filipino officials. 

Not after three weeks. 

Not after a senator begged them to act. Immediately. 

The response should have been clear: the Vienna Convention prohibits foreign diplomats from interfering in the internal affairs of the host state. 

Tarriela, Pangilinan, De Lima, and Diokno were performing their lawful duties. 

China's demands for "accountability" are out of line, and the Philippines will not tolerate interference.

The Palace should have issued a statement defending Filipino officials. 

Not silence. 

Not a press briefing about peso exchange rates. 

A clear, public defense of sovereignty and the right of Filipino officials to speak the truth about Chinese aggression.

The visa-free policy should have been tied to reciprocity and de-escalation. 

Not announced in the middle of harassment incidents. 

Not framed as a unilateral gift with no conditions. If China wants visa-free access for its nationals, it should offer the same to Filipinos. And it should stop harassing our fishermen.

We should summon the Chinese ambassador and make clear that public attacks on Filipino officials are unacceptable. 

Reciprocity matters. If they summon our ambassador, we summon theirs.

 HOLDING THEM ACCOUNTABLE

You're right to feel the injustice, VY. Every Filipino should.

We're watching our government stay silent while a foreign power attacks our officials, harasses our fishermen, and disrespects our sovereignty. 

We're watching them offer concessions with no reciprocity, no conditions, and no backbone.

And you're right to demand accountability. 

Because accountability starts with asking questions. 

With refusing to accept vague statements and radio silence. 

With demanding that our leaders defend not just our territory, but the dignity of the Filipino officials who risk their careers to speak the truth.

Tarriela refused to apologize. He said, "Transparency exposes bullies." He's right. And the rest of our government should follow his lead.

Because if we don't push back now, if we don't demand better from our leaders, then we're accepting that this is normal. 

That foreign embassies can attack our officials. 

That our fishermen can be driven from their waters. That sovereignty is just a word we say but don't mean.

And that's not the Philippines I want to live in.

Maraming salamat for writing, VY. For caring enough to ask. For refusing to accept the silence.

That's how we hold them accountable. By refusing to stay silent ourselves.

God bless you and your family, too.

Gino, MCT

CAN FREEDOM OF EXPRESSION REALLY SAVE YOU FROM SEDITION CHARGES?

When I heard Kiko Barzaga was using "freedom of expression" as his shield against an inciting to sedition case, I got curious. 

Is that even a thing? Can you really hide behind free speech when the state says you're stirring people up against the government? 

I wanted a straight answer, so I went back to the Constitution, the Revised Penal Code, and old Supreme Court decisions to see where the line actually is.

What I found is simple but inconvenient: freedom of expression can protect you, but it cannot rescue every stupid or reckless thing a politician says. Especially when that politician is old enough to know better.

 WHAT THE CONSTITUTION ACTUALLY GUARANTEES

Let's start with what's solid.

Article III, Section 4 of the 1987 Constitution says no law shall be passed abridging freedom of speech, of expression, of the press, or the right of the people peaceably to assemble and petition the government for redress of grievances. 

That's the bedrock. Political speech sits at the top of that protection. 

Criticizing government, calling out officials, questioning policies—lahat 'yan, may malakas na proteksyon.

The Supreme Court has said again and again that if the state wants to restrict speech, especially political speech, it has to pass a very high bar. 

In Chavez v. Gonzales, the Court used the "clear and present danger" test and said the harm must be extremely serious and the danger extremely imminent before speech can be punished.

Pero eto ang hindi laging sinasabi sa soundbites: that protection is strong, but not unlimited. Hindi siya "say anything, do anything, then just shout freedom of speech."

 WHAT SEDITION AND INCITING TO SEDITION ACTUALLY MEAN

Now, let's talk about the thing chasing Barzaga: inciting to sedition.

Under Article 139 of the Revised Penal Code, sedition happens when people rise publicly and tumultuously, by force, intimidation, or other illegal means, to block the law, obstruct government functions, or inflict acts of hate or revenge on public officials or their property.

Article 142 then punishes people who don't directly join the sedition, but incite others to do it. 

How? Through speeches, writings, publications, banners, cartoons, or any form of expression. It also covers seditious words or "scurrilous libels" against the government that:

- Disturb or obstruct public officers in their duties
- Instigate unlawful assemblies
- Incite conspiracies or riots
- Stir up people against lawful authorities
- Disturb the peace, safety, and order of the government

So yes, words can be a crime in our law. Not all words, but certain words, in certain contexts, with a certain intent.

That's the world Barzaga is playing in.

 THE CLEAR AND PRESENT DANGER TEST: THE REAL FILTER

The big question is: when do words cross from protected speech into criminal incitement?

The Supreme Court's main filter is the clear and present danger test. This means speech can only be punished if it presents:

- A clear danger
- A present (imminent) danger
- Of a serious evil that the state has the right and duty to prevent

Hindi puwede yung "baka someday may mangyari." The danger must be real, specific, and proximate. 

The Court has said the evil must be "extremely serious" and the degree of imminence "extremely high" before expression can be restrained or punished.

There is another standard called the dangerous tendency test, which basically asks if the speech tends to lead to a bad result at some point. 

Mas madali 'yun gamitin laban sa speech, kaya over the years, the Court has leaned more on clear and present danger in free speech cases.

So when someone like Barzaga says something wild, the legal question is not "offensive ba?" but "does it create a clear and present danger of people actually committing sedition or similar crimes?"

 WHAT PAST CASES TELL US ABOUT THE LINE

The old cases are brutal, but they're useful.

In Espuelas v. People, a man faked a suicide note addressed to the nation. 

In that note, he attacked the Roxas administration as "dirty" and compared officials to Hitlers and Mussolinis. He also implied violent acts and encouraged hateful attitudes towards government officials. 

The Supreme Court convicted him of inciting to sedition. 

The Court said his words had a natural tendency to stir disloyalty and hostility toward the government, going beyond ordinary criticism.

In People v. Feleo, the speaker praised the Russian revolution and openly urged the overthrow of the Philippine government and its replacement by a Soviet-style regime. 

The Court upheld his conviction. 

When you call for overthrowing constitutional government through force, you leave the shelter of free speech.

These cases say a few things clearly:

- Criticizing government acts and officials is protected.
- Calling for violent overthrow or assassination is not.
- Fake "dramas" that push people toward hate and violence against government can be punished.

And honestly, they should serve as a warning to politicians who love drama but hate reading.

 WHERE BARZAGA'S CASE FITS

Let's zero in on him.

Kiko Barzaga is facing complaints for inciting to sedition and for holding a rally without a permit. 

One complaint is tied to his commentary about Mindanao secession. Another to a Forbes Park rally. His defense line? It's all freedom of expression.

He says:
- He was just speaking his mind on political issues.
- The rally did not turn violent.
- There was no disorder.

Those facts matter. If no one got hurt, no one rioted, no one stormed any building, it becomes harder to argue there was clear and present danger. It supports his claim that what he did stayed within political speech, not criminal incitement.

But prosecutors will not stop at "walang gulo." They will look at:

- Exact words he used
- The way he framed secession
- How he addressed his audience
- Whether his statements, in context, were reasonably likely to push people toward illegal acts

Under the DOJ's newer standard, they're supposed to file cases only when there is "reasonable certainty of conviction," which means they must see a clear path to proving all elements of the crime beyond reasonable doubt. 

So for inciting to sedition, they must show:

- He made particular statements
- Those statements fall under Article 142
- He intended to incite people to commit sedition
- His speech created a clear and present danger of that happening

If his words are read as political criticism and advocacy (even strong, even irresponsible), that helps him. If they look like a reckless flirtation with sedition themes without real calls to illegal action, that still helps him. If they sound like direct invitations to unconstitutional acts, he's in trouble.

 WHAT COUNTS AS PROTECTED POLITICAL SPEECH

Based on the Constitution and cases, here's the kind of speech that usually stays protected:

- Criticizing the President, Congress, or any official
- Saying government is corrupt, incompetent, or abusive
- Calling for resignation, impeachment, or electoral defeat
- Advocating for autonomy, federalism, or even drastic policy shifts, as long as you push it through legal processes
- Organizing and joining peaceful rallies and assemblies
- Using strong language, sarcasm, anger, as part of political commentary

Lawyers for groups like the United People's Initiative have argued that even widespread calls for a president to resign are part of democratic discourse, not sedition, as long as they are peaceful and rooted in political belief. 

The Constitution itself says no person shall be detained solely by reason of political beliefs and aspirations.

So the law gives wide breathing room for dissent. 

Hindi bawal magalit. Hindi bawal maging maingay. Hindi bawal mag-demand ng resignation.

What it really watches out for is something else.

 WHAT PULLS YOU OUTSIDE FREE SPEECH PROTECTION

Speech becomes legally dangerous when it moves into:

- Explicit calls for violence against officials or institutions
- Advocacy of armed rebellion or coups, instead of elections or legal reforms
- Words designed to trigger immediate lawless action, not just long-term political change
- Scurrilous attacks (fake, malicious claims) combined with calls to take illegal steps against government

The Court, and the law, care about:

- Intent (gusto mo ba talagang mag-udyok?)
- Content (ano mismo ang sinabi mo?)
- Context (saan, kailan, kanino mo sinabi?)
- Effect (nagkaroon ba ng tunay na panganib o aksyon?)

If your words stay inside democratic tools—elections, protests, petitions, legal reforms—you are safer. The more you flirt with force, guns, secession by threat, the shakier your "freedom of expression" defense becomes.

 THE PROBLEM OF WEAPONIZING SEDITION

Now here's the part that makes everything messy: sedition laws have a history of being used as political weapons.

Originally, sedition laws in the Philippines were used by colonial powers against Filipino nationalists. 

After independence, those laws stayed. Modern governments have been accused of using sedition charges to harass critics, activists, and opposition figures.

You can see patterns:

- Opposition voices get sedition complaints faster.
- Allies who use equally harsh or irresponsible language walk free.

That's why the clear and present danger test is so important. It forces the state to justify each case with real evidence, not just political irritation. It also explains why many sedition or inciting to sedition cases either get dropped or struggle in court once judges start asking, "Where is the actual danger?"

Which brings me back to people like Barzaga.

 READ AND RESEARCH BEFORE YOU OPEN YOUR MOUTH

After going through the Constitution, the Revised Penal Code, and the old cases, the answer looks like this:

Freedom of expression can protect you from sedition and inciting to sedition charges, but only if what you said stays within the bounds of protected political speech. 

Criticism, even sharp and angry, is protected. Calls for change through legal, democratic means are protected. Peaceful protest is protected.

What is not protected are words that seriously and imminently push people toward violence, rebellion, or unconstitutional acts. 

The clear and present danger test is supposed to guard that line. It demands that the state prove your words are not just offensive or noisy, but actually dangerous in a real, immediate way.

That’s why people like Barzaga matter here. When a sitting congressman leans on “freedom of expression” without showing he understands its limits, he doesn’t just risk his own case. He muddies the water for everyone else who might need that protection in good faith: activists, journalists, students, ordinary citizens.

So my message is simple:

Read and research before you open your mouth.

Use the protections of the Constitution, but don’t abuse them.
Because every time a politician treats “freedom of expression” like a get-out-of-jail-free card for careless speech, it makes it easier for those in power to question that freedom for the rest of us.

You want to invoke the Constitution? Fine.

Then speak like someone who knows it, not someone who only remembers it when the subpoena arrives.
